Cybertruck Gas Station
I had to stop and ask what’s going on here. He works for Santa Clara county to do gas station leak checks. Cybertruck saves him $30K in fuel costs per year versus a standard utility truck. And he loves the 110V plug in back instead of hauling a generator. https://t.co/9Blne9uAmf

The comparisons are immediate and stark—when we drove past Waymo and Zoox vehicles outfitted with their complex sensor suite, they stuck out like a sore thumb. In contrast, the robotaxis blended in with other Teslas on the road; we felt inconspicuous flowing with the traffic. Confirming our thesis, robotaxi was half the price of Uber, showing its ability to win market share by weaponizing price. In Austin we took multiple robotaxi and Waymo rides; the contrast was clear. Aside from the visual difference between each pulling up to the curb, the robotaxi was comfortable and familiar, and it felt as though a friendly ghost chauffeur was driving our personal car. Driving was smooth and human-like, recognizing and patiently waiting for pedestrians, switching into less crowded lanes, patiently waiting to execute a safe unprotected turn, and yet, discerning and confident enough to drive through a light that just turned yellow, so as not to slam on the brakes. Waymo also provided a top-notch service and we did not encounter any safety concerns, but if we were to be overly critical, if felt more ... robotic. In the cabin you have to listen to an airline-esque preamble on Waymo and safety protocols, and during the ride you can hear all the various spinning lidar sensors spooling up and down with electronic whizzing sounds. In short, robotaxi felt like a more luxurious service for half the cost and the driving felt more human-like.""}}}, "rest_id": "1957811287343186335", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "5476705", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "1958174443844288987", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "5246501", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

{"items": [{"media_urls": [], "message": "Where is the death of Achilles described?"}, {"deepsearch_headers": [], "media_urls": [], "message": "The death of Achilles is not described in Homer's *Iliad*, which focuses primarily on a few weeks of the Trojan War and ends with the funeral of Hector, before Achilles' own demise. Instead, his death is detailed in later works and traditions from the Epic Cycle, a collection of ancient Greek poems that expand on the Trojan War narrative beyond the *Iliad* and *Odyssey*. ### Key Sources Describing Achilles' Death: 1. **Aethiopis** (part of the Epic Cycle): - This lost epic, attributed to Arctinus of Miletus (circa 776 BCE), is the primary source for Achilles' death. It describes how, after the death of Hector, Achilles kills the Amazon warrior Penthesilea and the Ethiopian king Memnon. Subsequently, Paris (with the aid of the god Apollo) shoots Achilles with an arrow in his heel—the only vulnerable part of his body due to his mother Thetis' dipping him in the River Styx. The wound proves fatal, and Achilles dies at Troy. - Fragments and summaries of the *Aethiopis* survive through later writers like Proclus (in his *Chrestomathy*), who outline the plot. 2. **Post-Homeric Literature**: - **Quintus of Smyrna's *Posthomerica* (4th century CE)**: This later epic, written in Greek, provides a detailed narrative of Achilles' death. It recounts Paris shooting the arrow guided by Apollo, striking Achilles' heel, leading to his collapse and eventual death. The text also describes the mourning of the Greeks and the subsequent battle for his body. - **Ovid's *Metamorphoses* (Book 12, 8 CE)**: The Roman poet Ovid briefly mentions Achilles' death, linking it to the heel vulnerability and Paris' arrow, though with less detail than the Greek sources. - **Statius' *Achilleid* (1st century CE)**: This unfinished Latin epic alludes to Achilles' destiny but does not cover his death, focusing instead on his early life. 3. **Other References**: - In Homer's *Odyssey* (Book 11, the Nekyia), Odysseus encounters Achilles' shade in the Underworld, confirming his death but not detailing how it occurred. This suggests the event was already part of the oral tradition by the time the *Odyssey* was composed (circa 1200 BCE). - Later Greek playwrights, such as Aeschylus (*Myrmidons*, now lost) and Euripides (*Iphigenia at Aulis*), reference Achilles' death indirectly, reinforcing the heel vulnerability myth. ### Context and Interpretation: - The story of Achilles' death, particularly the heel vulnerability, likely evolved as a narrative device to explain his otherwise invincible nature, tied to the myth of Thetis' attempt to immortalize him. Archaeological evidence from Troy (Hisarlik, Turkey) and Mycenaean sites shows warriors wore protective gear, but no specific "Achilles heel" artifact exists—suggesting the detail is mythological rather than historical. - As of 2025, ongoing debates among classicists (e.g., via journals like *Classical Quarterly*) explore whether the heel story was a later addition, possibly influenced by Near Eastern tales of heroic flaws, given cultural exchanges during the Late Bronze Age. In summary, Achilles' death is most comprehensively described in the *Aethiopis* and elaborated in later works like Quintus' *Posthomerica*, building on the oral traditions predating Homer. These texts, preserved through fragments and summaries, remain the cornerstone for understanding this iconic moment in Greek mythology."}]}, "is_translatable": false, "legacy": {"bookmark_count": 37, "bookmarked": false, "conversation_id_str": "1956913475784990833", "created_at": "Sun Aug 17 16:40:50 +0000 2025", "display_text_range": [16, 39], "entities": {"hashtags": [], "symbols": [], "timestamps": [], "urls": [{"display_url": "x.com/i/grok/share/5…", "expanded_url": "https://x.com/i/grok/share/5rcqWzsQsDsj5M4JpsuhzzA1x", "indices": [16, 39], "url": "https://t.co/DCcnTdxQkj"}], "user_mentions": [{"id_str": "1541478309623730180", "indices": [0, 15], "name": "Roman Helmet Guy", "screen_name": "romanhelmetguy"}]}, "favorite_count": 514, "favorited": false, "full_text": "@romanhelmetguy https://t.co/DCcnTdxQkj", "id_str": "1957120447579054103", "in_reply_to_screen_name": "romanhelmetguy", "in_reply_to_status_id_str": "1956913475784990833", "in_reply_to_user_id_str": "1541478309623730180", "is_quote_status": false, "lang": "qme", "possibly_sensitive": false, "possibly_sensitive_editable": true, "quote_count": 5, "reply_count": 124, "retweet_count": 45, "retweeted": false, "user_id_str": "44196397"}, "rest_id": "1957120447579054103", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "42347", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

embedded_text:
I have been coding the past week, just using Grok.

This is the first time I’ve been able to rely on AI for 100% of the code.

Never been able to do that before. Not with ChatGPT or with Claude.

I was really surprised when I realized that none of the code was mine.

text:
They’re building the Hyperloop! 

Good for them. We should do it in America.
embedded_text:
China is testing a vacuum high-speed train that can travel over 1,000 km/h. 

This ultra-fast vacuum tube magnetic levitation system could reduce the travel time between Shanghai and Hangzhou (distance: 200 km) to just 9 minutes.

Grok is ranked #2 in Productivity, but it’s very hard to find in the AppStore.

Even lower-ranked apps are featured, but not Grok.

Why this bias, @Apple? https://t.co/zNnsUXVq6A

text:
The idea of Grok Imagine is to generate images as fast as your imagination, giving you many options to choose in exploration space, then zero in on a particular concept and animate rapidly, iterating many times in animation. 

This is just the early Beta release of Imagine …
embedded_text:
Create consistent characters with Grok Imagine - something that’s tough to achieve with any other AI but so much easier with Grok

Just describe your character and their actions, and Grok will generate multiple story scenes featuring that character in different scenarios

Create https://t.co/UM2FxvGRKe

Wait - your story doesn't have a comprehensive history/mythology spanning multiple ages all the way back to a creation myth as detailed in separate volumes? You didn't first invent new languages and dialects for your characters? You didn't pack it with powerful themes and stories written it in a beautiful, archaic style and compose poems and songs alongside? It didn't take you multiple decades of iteration? And what of all the uncharted territory still remaining? Is Tom Bombadil one of the Ainur. Where are the Entwives. What happened to the two unaccounted Istari. Can we hear more about what it was like in Cuiviénen when the elves first awoke? Or to see the light of the two trees of Valinor. Or of the splendor of the caves of Aglarond.\n\nWhat's most on my mind though - the Tolkien legendarium is imo a concrete example of a height of culture. Does AI, today or soon, make it easier to reach this high via empowerment in both writing and ideation? Or harder, when quick wins are tempting and ~free, and an independent ability to create is stifled. If such a body of work is made again but now with heavy AI assistance, does it inspire the same wonder? What if thousands of them come out on demand with just a prompt? Why do you feel cheated when you learn that something your read was AI generated? Is it transient or a function of capability? Is it slop? What is slop? Or is wonder inseparable from its own creation myth of a lifelong obsession of a mind like your own? text:
For the foreseeable future, the major AI companies will continue to prosper, as will @xAI. There is just so much to do!

Outside of real-world AI, Google has the biggest compute (and data) advantage for now, so currently has the highest probability of being the leader.

That may change in a few years.

text: The Tesla Robotaxi service area is already larger than any competitors in Austin and the Bay Area
embedded_text:
Tesla’s Robotaxi service in Austin launched just 55 days ago (not 4 months), and since then the geofenced area has already expanded 4x. The in-car safety monitor has limited controls to stop the vehicle if needed, but they are not actually driving it.
 
I’d much rather see Tesla https://t.co/qml8CabEqf

text: You can order Starlink, get it delivered and be online in under an hour
embedded_text:
Our home internet got cancelled because the people renting this house after us set their move-in date for two weeks before they were moving in and AT&T only lets you have one line per household.

After going back and forth endlessly I just Doordashed a Starlink.

embedded_text:
Grok on Tesla is super fun.

Can brainstorm, learn a new thing, or simply ask “what’s in the news today” and dig into a specific story.

I could see Tesla making an AI phone.

𝕏 is ranked #1 News app on the Apple AppStore. 🇺🇸

Here’s what Apple is featuring instead:

➤ Local News & Weather -  Rank 88
➤ Business Insider - Rank 115
➤ Reuters - Rank 45
➤ Bloomberg -  Rank 36
➤ WSJ - Rank 8

Why this bias against 𝕏? https://t.co/RAE5pZEusy

text:
The phone/computer will just become an edge node for AI, directly rendering pixels with no real operating system or apps in the traditional sense. 

There isn’t enough bandwidth to transmit video to all devices from the servers and there won’t always be good connectivity, so there still needs to be significant client-side AI compute.

Asked Siri to change the song: “Here’s what I found on the web.” 🤦‍♂️

Why do I even bother?

Little girl playing with her puppy

text: How to use @Grok Imagine to turn your photos into videos
embedded_text:
How to get still photos into videos. This photo is 10 years old. 
Open @grok and click on Create Videos. On the lower left square with a +, choose a photo. Uploading and Generating Video appear. Then post on X.  Imagine 😍😍 https://t.co/Z8ymSvDPIr

text:
To be clear, I mean solar+batteries, not just solar. 

The entire Starlink constellation of over 7000 satellites, which go through Earth shadow many times per day, are just powered by solar+batteries.

And Earth is just a giant satellite.

Biggest difference when coding with GROK 4 by @xai compared to Claude 4 or GPT-5:

Claude and GPT-5 are eager, producing more code for UI embellishments or extra features.

Grok 4 is minimalistic, generating exactly what you specify. This is better in the long run. https://t.co/VkcMDgCLLu
Grok 4 is the best model for low-level C & ASM right now, and Grok Code is coming soon! 🔥

text: Will be interesting to see how this is used
embedded_text:
Starlink announced a $5/month plan that gives unlimited usage at 500kbits/s.  

Modern apps and web pages would immediately back up every buffer and make for a painful experience, but it is fun to consider optimizing inside that tight box (in the 90s, 4x ISDN was high end!).

Modern apps and web pages would immediately back up every buffer and make for a painful experience, but it is fun to consider optimizing inside that tight box (in the 90s, 4x ISDN was high end!). With Starlink’s good latency, input-distribution multiplayer games would still work fine, as long as they didn’t download anything. You could even have voice chat. With an optimal implementation, you could scroll the X feed as fast as you want with full text, with progressive images coming in when you pause, but instantly ceasing bandwidth use as you start scrolling again. Remote shells would work well by default, but we could do a lot better than standard ANSI for complex updates. Server-rendered web pages or apps could at least be progressively rendered and text-first, with full responsiveness even when the fidelity is low."}}}, "rest_id": "1956499838809346279", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "15564464", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "1956502309560844674", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "14219361", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}
